questions.question: Midway through a shift on a wet horizontal bench, every part shows a strong fluorescent background and the particles are clumping. Parts have been arriving straight from machining, still wet with cutting fluid. The most likely root cause is:
options: The UV lamp intensity has drifted too high
Inadequate precleaning — oils carried into the bath are degrading the suspension
The current type was switched from AC to HWDC
The settling test was performed too recently
questions.explanation: Oily parts contaminate the bath, and fluorescent background plus clumping are classic contamination symptoms. Precleaning protects every test that follows, and shift bath checks exist to catch exactly this.

questions.question: Which precleaning method dissolves oils and greases effectively but will not remove rust or tightly adherent scale?
options: Acid pickling
Vapor degreasing
Abrasive blasting
Tumbling
questions.explanation: Vapor degreasing is a solvent method — hot solvent vapor condenses on the part and continuously rinses it. It is excellent on oils and greases but has no effect on oxide scale or rust.

questions.question: Tightly adherent mill scale must come off a hot-rolled bar before examination. Which precleaning method is actually matched to that contaminant?
options: Solvent hand wipe
Vapor degreasing
Acid pickling
Warm-air drying
questions.explanation: Tight scale requires chemical attack such as acid pickling (or aggressive mechanical work like blasting, with its own cautions). Solvent methods only remove what dissolves, and drying removes nothing.

questions.question: Under typical procedures, the surface that must be cleaned and examined extends how far beyond the specific area of interest?
options: No margin — only the exact area of interest
About one inch on all sides
At least one foot on all sides
Half the part's overall length
questions.explanation: Typical practice cleans and examines roughly an inch beyond the area of interest on all sides, so discontinuities at the edge of the zone are still judged on a valid, prepared surface.

questions.question: A prod examination is planned at 7-inch spacing where the procedure calls for about 100 amperes per inch of spacing. What is the typical current, and why must the prod tips bear on bare metal?
options: About 100 A total, because spacing does not affect amperage — bare metal is only cosmetic
About 700 A — scale or paint at the tips raises contact resistance, inviting arc strikes and unreliable current delivery into the part
About 70 A — a little scale actually improves electrical contact
About 7,000 A — paint must be removed so it can withstand the heat
questions.explanation: Typical procedure values run on the order of 90 to 125 amperes per inch of prod spacing, so 7 inches is roughly 700 A. Dirty contacts arc, and an arc strike's heat-affected zone can crack hardened material.

questions.question: A part arrives with 5 mils of intact paint, and the customer asks that the paint stay on. Under common practices, examination through the coating is permissible only if:
options: The paint is light-colored, to provide particle contrast
It is demonstrated — for example, with a shim-type artificial flaw under an equivalent nonmagnetic standoff — that the required sensitivity is achieved through the measured coating thickness
The amperage is doubled to compensate for the coating
The residual method is substituted for the continuous method
questions.explanation: Beyond a thin film, common practices put the burden on the examiner to prove detection through the coating, typically by showing a QQI-type reference flaw still indicates under a standoff equal to the measured maximum thickness.

questions.question: Nickel plating differs from ordinary paint as a coating over the examination surface because nickel:
options: Is transparent to UV-A light
Can be ferromagnetic — it carries flux, can mask leakage from flaws beneath it, and can crack itself
Is an electrical insulator
Always improves sensitivity by smoothing the surface
questions.explanation: Paint is simply a nonmagnetic standoff, but a ferromagnetic plating like nickel participates magnetically: it can short-circuit or mask leakage from the substrate and can produce indications of its own.

questions.question: During a bench examination, a crisp, dense, tightly held straight-line indication appears on a ground surface and re-forms in the same place after a gentle wipe and re-application under magnetization. It should be treated as:
options: A false indication from bath drainage
A surface-breaking discontinuity such as a grinding crack
A deep subsurface inclusion
Magnetic writing from part-to-part contact
questions.explanation: Sharp, dense, tightly held, and reproducible in place is the signature of a surface-breaking discontinuity. Subsurface flaws give broad fuzzy indications, while drainage patterns and magnetic writing do not re-form identically under proper re-magnetization.

questions.question: Why does complete MT coverage of an area require two magnetizations in roughly perpendicular directions?
options: The second shot demagnetizes the residual field left by the first

Leakage strong enough to hold particles forms only when flux crosses a discontinuity at roughly 45 to 90 degrees, so any single field direction is nearly blind to discontinuities lying close to parallel with the flux
Two shots double the flux density available at the surface
Standards require two directions only when fluorescent particles are used
questions.explanation: A discontinuity disturbs the flux only where the flux crosses it — best at 45 to 90 degrees. One field direction therefore misses flaws aligned near-parallel to the flux, and a second, roughly perpendicular magnetization closes that blind spot.

questions.question: For near-surface inclusions roughly an eighth of an inch deep in a rough-surfaced weldment, the classic technique pairing is:
options: AC with wet fluorescent particles
AC with dry powder
HWDC with dry powder, continuous method
The residual method with visible wet particles
questions.explanation: HWDC penetrates below the surface while its pulsation keeps dry powder mobile — the field standard for subsurface capability on rough work. AC's skin effect confines the field to the surface, and the residual method is reserved for high-retentivity parts at lower sensitivity.
